summary:
The Destruction of Carthage (146 BC) was the culminating act of the [[event:1058|Third Punic War]] — the systematic demolition of one of the ancient world's greatest cities and the enslavement or killing of its entire population, eliminating at a stroke the last power capable of rivaling Rome in the western Mediterranean. The destruction has been treated by historians as a turning point in Roman history, marking the transition from pragmatic imperialism to something more brutal and absolute.

After three years of siege and street fighting, Scipio Aemilianus's forces broke through Carthage's final defenses in 146 BC. A week of house-to-house fighting reduced the city quarter by quarter; ancient sources describe flames and collapsing buildings burying combatants on both sides. Approximately 50,000 survivors were sold into slavery. The city was systematically demolished; Scipio reportedly wept as he watched it burn, allegedly quoting Homer on the fall of Troy and reflecting that Rome might someday face the same fate. The territory became the Roman province of Africa.

The destruction of Carthage in 146 BC coincided with the Roman sack of Corinth in the same year, following the [[event:1064|Roman Conquest of Greece]], creating the symbolic image of Rome simultaneously eliminating its two greatest cultural and military rivals. Rome emerged from 146 BC as the uncontested mistress of the Mediterranean world.
